coredata.dc:title: Electrochemical and optical dual-mode detection of phenolic compounds using MnO2/GQD nanozyme
coredata.dc:creator: Facure, Murilo H.M.
Andre, Rafaela S.
Cardoso, Rafael M.
Mercante, Luiza A.
Correa, Daniel S.
coredata.dc:description: Phenolic compounds (PCs) have been widely employed in industrial activities and have also been found at threatening levels in natural environments, posing risks to human health and the environment. As a consequence, there is an increasing demand for the development of simple and sensitive detection methods of such pollutants. In this work, we report the use of manganese dioxide and graphene quantum dot (MnO2/GQD) composites as a dual-functional sensing platform for the electrochemical and colorimetric detection of catechol (CC) and dopamine (DA), two important PCs. Regarding the electrochemical detection, differential pulse voltammetry showed a linear relationship between current signal intensity and concentration of DA and CC over the linear ranges of 0.5–100 μM and 5–150 μM, with limits of detection of 0.05 µM and 0.09 µM for DA and CC, respectively. In addition, the composite was employed for the colorimetric detection of CC and DA through the oxidase mimic activity of the MnO2/GQD in the presence of 3,5,3,5-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB). Analysis in real samples showed that the MnO2/GQD composite can be used for the sensitive detection of CC and DA in river samples. The high surface area and improved electrochemical properties of the MnO2/GQD were demonstrated to be beneficial for dual sensing applications of phenolic compounds.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemical detection
Colorimetric detection
Nanozyme
Catechol
Dopamine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Stability, selectivity and real sample analysis

Coordination of Higher Education Personnel Improvement
National Council for Scientific and Technological Development

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors thank the financial support from FAPESP ( 2018/22214-6 , 2017/10582-8 , 2020/14946-7 ), CNPq ( 150002/2021-1 ), MCTI-SisNano ( CNPq/402.287/2013-4 ), Coordenação de Aperfeiçoamento de Pessoal de Nível Superior - Brasil (CAPES) - Código de Financiamento 001, and Rede Agronano (EMBRAPA). The authors also thank the Laboratory of Structural Characterization (LCE/DEMa/UFSCar) for the microscopy facilities and Dr. Marcelo B. Andrade (IFSC-USP) for the Raman measurements.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FEG-SEM images of (a, b) MnO2 and (c, d) MnO2/GQD composite at different magnifications.
(a) FITR spectra of MnO2/GQD, MnO2, and GQD and (b) Raman spectra of MnO2 and MnO2/GQD.
(a) CVs and (b) Nyquist plots of EIS of bare SPE and SPE modified with MnO2/GQD in a 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− solution with 0.1 M KCl.

CV curves of 50 μM (a) DA and (c) CC using the MnO2/GQD modified electrode in 2 M HAc-NaAc buffer solution (pH 4.0) at different scan rates. Linear relationships of the Ipa and Ipc with the (b) square root of the scan rate for DA and (d) with the scan rate for CC.

DPV curves for crescent concentrations of (a) DA and (c) CC and the corresponding linear regression curves (b and d) obtained by the measured DPV peak heights. HAc-NaAc buffer solution (pH 4.0). DPV optimal conditions: scan rate of 10 mV s−1, pulse amplitude of 25 mV, step of 5 mV, and pulse width (or modulation time) of 0.5 s.

UV-vis spectra of the TMB solution collected 5 min after the addition of (a) DA and (c) CC, and (b and d) the corresponding linear regression curves obtained with the variation of the absorbance (ΔA) at 652 nm.

(a) Stability of the MnO2/GQD sensor during two weeks for the detection of DA and CC and (b) the response for CC, DA, and interferents (AA: ascorbic acid, Glu: Glucose, Urea, BPA: bisphenol A, NaCl, KCl, and MgCl2) applying the detection methods used for CC (green) and DA (red).
Schematic illustration of the (a) electrochemical and (b) colorimetric detection of catechol (CC) and dopamine (DA) using the MnO2/GQD composite with oxidase-like activity.
Comparison between the colorimetric and the electrochemical detection towards CC and DA using the MnO2/GQD nanozyme with other works reported in the literature.
Electrochemical detection of DA and CC in real water samples.

head.ce:title: Electrochemical and optical dual-mode detection of phenolic compounds using MnO2/GQD nanozyme

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemistry Institute, Federal University of Bahia (UFBA), Salvador, BA 40170-115, Brazil

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Nanotechnology National Laboratory for Agriculture (LNNA), Embrapa Instrumentação, Sao Carlos, SP 13560-970, Brazil.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Phenolic compounds (PCs) have been widely employed in industrial activities and have also been found at threatening levels in natural environments, posing risks to human health and the environment. As a consequence, there is an increasing demand for the development of simple and sensitive detection methods of such pollutants. In this work, we report the use of manganese dioxide and graphene quantum dot (MnO2/GQD) composites as a dual-functional sensing platform for the electrochemical and colorimetric detection of catechol (CC) and dopamine (DA), two important PCs. Regarding the electrochemical detection, differential pulse voltammetry showed a linear relationship between current signal intensity and concentration of DA and CC over the linear ranges of 0.5–100 μM and 5–150 μM, with limits of detection of 0.05 µM and 0.09 µM for DA and CC, respectively. In addition, the composite was employed for the colorimetric detection of CC and DA through the oxidase mimic activity of the MnO2/GQD in the presence of 3,5,3,5-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB). Analysis in real samples showed that the MnO2/GQD composite can be used for the sensitive detection of CC and DA in river samples. The high surface area and improved electrochemical properties of the MnO2/GQD were demonstrated to be beneficial for dual sensing applications of phenolic compounds.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Phenolic compounds (PCs) are molecules that contain one or more hydroxyl groups bonded to aromatic rings, which can be found in animal and human fluids as well as in natural environments [1,2]. Such compounds have also been widely employed in many industrial processes due to some appealing properties such as antioxidant capacity, anti-inflammatory, and antimicrobial activities [1,3]. However, many PCs and their derivatives present toxicity and are hardly degraded, which makes them major environmental pollutants whose presence in natural environments and biological systems can pose significant threats to their normal functioning [1,2]. In this way, detecting PCs in a reliable, fast, and sensitive way is very important for adopting adequate measures for mitigating their harmful impact [3–5].

PCs have been detected by different techniques, including liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) [6], ultra-performance liquid chromatography (UPLC) [7], and liquid chromatography coupled to ultraviolet detection (RP-HPLC/UV) [8]. However, for practical and fast applications, such techniques have limitations, as they require laborious processes, sophisticated equipment, and specific knowledge for the interpretation of results. In this scenario, electrochemical detection stands out as a sensitive and fast technique that allows the miniaturization of devices for in situ practical analysis [4,5,9,10].

When it comes to electrochemical detection of PCs, higher sensitivity and selectivity can be achieved by modifying the working electrode with enzymes capable to catalyze the redox reactions [11–13]. However, natural enzymes can be expensive, present poor stability, and demand complicated processes to be immobilized onto the electrode surface [14,15]. As an alternative, nanomaterials that present enzyme-like activities, i.e., nanozymes, can be used in the electrochemical detection of PCs [16–18]. Besides the catalytic mimicking properties, the nanomaterial can be tailored to present desired properties, e.g., high conductivity and large specific surface area, which will be beneficial to attain enhanced performances. Also, the colorimetric detection of the analytes can be performed using specific nanozymes combined to chromogenic substrates [15,19,20].

In this work, we report the use of a composite containing manganese dioxide and graphene quantum dot (MnO2/GQD) that presents oxidase-like activity to detect two PCs: catechol (CC) and dopamine (DA). CC has been widely used in many industrial sectors and is toxic to humans, animals, and plants [21]. DA is a neurotransmitter whose high levels have been associated with neurological diseases [4,22]. The electrochemical detection of both analytes was performed and compared with colorimetric detection, as illustrated in Scheme 1. While the colorimetric strategy enables instantaneous detection through color changes of the solution containing the analyte, the electrochemical detection provided a wider linear range and superior sensitivity.

Potassium permanganate (KMnO4) and graphite were obtained from Dinamica, Brazil. Sodium hydroxide, ethanol, sodium acetate, acetic acid, sulfuric acid, hydrochloric acid, potassium ferricyanide (K3Fe(CN)6), and potassium ferrocyanide (K4Fe(CN)6) were purchased from Synth. 3,5,3′,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), CC, and DA were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. All aqueous solutions were prepared using double-distilled water.

The MnO2/GQD composite was synthesized by an adapted hydrothermal method, according to our previous work [20]. In the procedure, 5 mL of the GQD solution was mixed with 15 mL of water and 20 mL of ethanol containing 0.5 g of KMnO4. For comparison, the synthesis of the MnO2 counterpart was performed without adding the GQD solution and using 20 mL of water. The homogeneous solution was heated (5 °C min−1) in a 50 mL autoclave to 100 °C and kept at this temperature for 1.5 h. A dialysis tube (2000 Da MWCO) was used to remove the unreacted reagents and salts present in the solution. After dialysis, the solvent was evaporated in an oven at 50 °C to obtain the MnO2/GQD powder, which was grounded with pestle and mortar.

Field Emission Gun Scanning Electron Microscopy (FEG-SEM) images were obtained using a JEOL-JSM 6701F. The samples were prepared by casting the solution containing the materials onto silicon wafers. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was performed on a Bruker Vertex 70 in the ATR mode, collecting 64 scans at a 2 cm−1 resolution from 4000 to 400 cm−1. Raman spectroscopy analyses were carried out with a LabRAM HR Evolution Raman spectrometer using an excitation wavelength at 532 nm, which spectra were collected from 200 to 1600 cm−1. FTIR and Raman measurements were performed with the materials as powders. The porosity of the as-prepared materials was measured by N2 adsorption/desorption experiments using an ASAP-2020 system (Micromeritics). The specific surface area was calculated from the N2 adsorption data according to the Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) method.

Commercial screen-printed carbon electrodes (SPE) (DropSens) were used in the electrochemical measurements. The modification of the working electrodes was performed by drop-casting 10 µL of the MnO2/GQD (1 mg mL−1) dispersion onto the electrode's surface. Prior to the working electrode modification, the suspension was sonicated for 5 min using a water bath sonicator.

All electrochemical experiments were performed using a potentiostat/galvanostat PGSTAT30 Autolab (Metrohm) controlled by Nova software (version 2.1). Cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were conducted in a 5 mM [Fe(CN)]3−/4− solution containing 0.1 M KCl as supporting electrolyte. CV experiments were performed over a potential range from −0.4 to 0.6 V at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1. EIS measurements were carried out at open circuit potential (OCP) over the frequency range from 0.1 Hz to 100 kHz, using an amplitude of 10 mV. Differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) measurements were performed in acetic acid-sodium acetate buffer (HAc-NaAc) (0.2 M, pH 4.0) in the potential range from -0.1 to 0.4 V using a pulse amplitude of 25 mV, a step potential of 5 mV, modulation time of 0.5 s at a scan rate of 10 mV s−1. Real sample analyses were performed using filtered bottled water, tap water and river water (collected from the Monjolinho river, located in São Carlos, SP, Brazil).

The colorimetric detection was performed based on the oxidase-mimicking catalytic activity of the as-prepared nanozyme (MnO2/GQD) using a Shimadzu UV-1900 spectrometer, as follows: 20 μL of the nanozyme suspension (1 mg mL−1) and 200 μL of TMB (2 mM) were added to the HAc-NaAc buffer solution. After 5 min of incubation, different concentrations of CC or DA were added to the blue solution and UV−vis absorption spectra were recorded at each 30 s for 5 min. The calibration curve was obtained by the absorbance difference (ΔA = A0 - A5, where A0 and A5 are the absorbance values at 652 nm at 0 and 5 min respectively) caused by the addition of the analytes.

FEG-SEM images of the grounded products are shown in Fig. 1 . It can be observed that for both MnO2 and MnO2/GQD the morphology of the MnO2 is predominantly amorphous, with some nanowire crystals being found in smaller amounts. The larger sizes presented a few micrometers in length.

Fig. 2 a presents the FTIR spectra of the synthesized materials. In the MnO2 spectrum, the peaks at 418 and 497 cm−1 are related to the characteristic vibrations of MnO bonds, while the peaks at 747 and 1420 cm−1 are ascribed to the vibrations of OMnO bonding of α-MnO2. The peaks at 1343 and 1633 cm−1 are respectively associated with MnOH and HOH vibrations from the water molecule adsorbed onto the surface of the material [23,24]. In comparison to MnO2, the FTIR spectrum of MnO2/GQD presents additional peaks in the range 800–1200 cm−1 while some of the peaks in the range 1200–1800 cm−1 are slightly shifted. The changes can be attributed to the presence of oxygen functional groups including hydroxyl, carbonyl, and carboxylic groups, and the CC bonds of the GQD carbon structure [25], thereby confirming the formation of the composite.

The Raman spectra presented in Fig. 2b show two main bands at 570 and 621 cm−1 related to the α-MnO2 phase and can be ascribed to the Mn−O symmetrical vibrations [26,27]. It was not possible to observe any Raman signal related to the presence of GQD, which may be explained by the low proportion of GQD in the composite. The Raman results revealed that the presence of the GQD in the hydrothermal reaction did not affect the synthesis of the α-MnO2.

Even in low concentrations, the addition of GQD led to a substantial increase in the BET specific surface area of the composite when compared to the neat MnO2, as shown in Fig. S1. The specific surface area of the MnO2/GQD was found to be 246.2 m2 g−1, which is ca. 2.6 times higher than that of neat MnO2 (94.6 m2 g−1). An increase in the specific surface area by combining GQD with other materials has also been reported in other works, which is highly desirable for electrochemical sensing applications [28–30].

The electrochemical behavior of the SPE electrode modified with MnO2/GQD was investigated by CV in [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−, as displayed in Fig. 3 a. For the bare SPE, a pair of well-defined redox peaks was observed, with a peak-to-peak (ΔEp) separation of 210 mV. The modification with the composite led to a redox current increase and a decrease in the ΔEp to 170 mV. The larger peak current and smaller ΔEp indicate that MnO2/GQD presents faster electron transfer kinetics between the redox probe solution and the electrode surface when compared with the bare electrode. Such behavior can be attributed to the unique properties of as-developed composite such as its superior surface area and electrical conductivity. Fig. 3b shows the Nyquist plots of bare SPE and SPE modified with MnO2/GQD, where the diameter of the semicircle is associated with the electron-transfer resistance (Rct). As shown, the Rct decreased substantially from 2390 to 1480 Ω after the electrode modification, implying enhanced electron transfer property and better mass transfer performance, which are consistent with the above results from CV [31,32].

CV was further used to investigate the electrochemical behavior of CC and DA at MnO2/GQD composite-modified electrode. In the presence of DA (Fig. S2a), a pair of well-defined redox peaks were observed at Epa = 252 mV and Epc = 170 mV, corresponding to the oxidation of DA to dopaminequinone (anodic peak) and reduction of dopaminequinone back to DA (cathodic peak) [33]. Similarly, the CV curves obtained in the presence of CC (Fig. S2b) are characterized by the presence of a pair of peaks at 202 mV and -40 mV, which are related to the redox reaction of CC to 1,2-benzoquinone on the electrode surface [34].

CV was also used to investigate the anodic and cathodic peak changes with varying scan rates. As shown in Fig. 4 , the redox peak current of DA and CC increases with the increase in scan rates, while the peak potentials are shifted towards more negative and positive direction upon increasing the scan rates. Specifically, for DA (Fig. 4a and b), Ipa and Ipc increase linearly with the square root of scan rate in the range from 20 to 200 mV/s, suggesting a typical diffusion-controlled electrochemical behavior of DA at the surface of the MnO2/GQD composite and a reversible electron transfer process [22,35]. For CC (Fig. 4c and d), on the other hand, the linear increase in Ipa and Ipc with the scan rate in the range from 10 to 120 mV indicates that the electrochemical reaction of CC at the MnO2/GQD interface is an adsorption-controlled process. The increase in the peak-to-peak separation (ΔEp) with the scan rate (150 mV at 10 mV s−1 to 330 mV at 120 mV s−1) indicates that the CC redox reaction is an electrochemically quasi-reversible process. Also, the larger potential window (0.6 V) and ΔEp (222 mV at 20 mV s−1) for the CC redox reaction compared to the process with DA (0.5 V and 63 mV at 20 mV s−1) corroborate with the quasi-reversibility and reversibility processes for CC and DA, respectively [35].

The relationship between the oxidation peak currents and the concentrations of CC and DA was further investigated by the DPV method, and the results are displayed in Fig. 5 . An increase in the peak current can be observed when the concentrations of DA (Fig. 5a) and CC (Fig. 5c) increase. As shown in Fig. 5b and d, the calibration plots confirm that the DPV peak heights are linearly dependent on the DA and CC concentrations over the range of 0.5–100 μM and 5–150 μM, respectively, yielding sensitivity values of 0.059 µA/µM for DA and 0.036 µA/µM for CC. The limit of detection (LOD) obtained by DPV was calculated by the 3σ/S ratio, in which S is the slope of the regression curve and σ is the standard deviation of the lowest concentration analyzed [22]. The LODs obtained were found to be 0.05 µM and 0.09 µM for DA and CC, respectively.

Fig. 6 a and c shows the UV-vis spectra of the TMB solution recorded after 5 min of addition of DA and CC in different concentrations. A linear relationship was observed between the ΔA and the amount of analyte added to the TMB solution, as shown in Fig. 6b and 6d for DA and CC, respectively. A linear range from 10 µM to 50 µM was obtained for DA detection, while for CC these values ranged from 4 µM to 40 µM. By using the 3σ/S ratio, the LOD values were found to be 0.95 µM for DA and 0.37 µM for CC.

Table 1 shows the comparison between the electrochemical and colorimetric detection approaches. As can be observed, the electrochemical detection provided lower LOD's and wider linear concentration ranges for both DA and CC. On the other hand, for detecting higher concentrations, colorimetric assays are advantageous for allowing easy and immediate visualization of the result by solution color changes in an equipment-free method, as shown in Fig. S3. Table 1 also presents the comparison of the sensing performances of this work with others reported in the literature. One can notice that the values obtained here are comparable or even superior to other recent results reported in the literature.

AuNPs: gold nanoparticles; h-CuS NCs: CuS nanozyme with hollow nanocube structure; CoFe2O4/CoS: CoFe2O4/CoS hybrid nanotubes; Cu-MOXs: Cu (II)-based metal-organic xerogels; Tyr-β-CD-AuNPs: tyrosinase immobilized on β-cyclodextrin-capped gold nanoparticles; Tyr-AuNPs-DHP/GCE: glassy carbon electrode modified with gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) and tyrosinase (Tyr) within a dihexadecylphosphate film; PA6/PAH_MWCNTs nanofibers: electrospun polyamide 6/poly(allylamine hydrochloride) nanofibers functionalized with multiwalled carbon nanotubes; CAuNE: cylindrical gold nanoelectrode; CuO nanodots-ITO: copper oxide nanodots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability, selectivity and real sample analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Taking advantage of the distinct electrocatalytic performance of the MnO2/GQD, the electrochemical method was chosen to evaluate the practical ability of the composite to be applied for the analysis of CC and DA in real water samples.

The stability of a MnO2/GQD modified electrode was tested during two weeks. As can be seen in Fig. 7
a, after 14 days the relative response decreased only by 7% and 13% for DA and CC, respectively. The selectivity of the sensor was also tested using some common interferents, namely, ascorbic acid, glucose, urea, bisphenol A, NaCl, KCl, and MgCl2. The results shown in the inset of Fig. 7b indicate that urea and the salts presented very low responses when using the detection methods for both CC and DA. The responses obtained for ascorbic acid and glucose were in the range of 35% and 55%.

The real sample analyses were performed using bottled, tap, and river water. The results shown in Table 2 reveal that the electrochemical detection of PCs using the MnO2/GQD nanozyme has potential to be performed in real media, which is important for monitoring these pollutants in real environmental samples.

Herein, we synthesized MnO2/GQD composite presenting oxidase-mimicking activities for sensing phenolic compounds. The characterization of the as-developed composite showed that the presence of GQD did not affect the production of MnO2 and led to a remarkable increase in the specific surface area of the material. Due to its advantageous properties, the composite was used in the electrochemical and colorimetric detection of phenolic compounds, namely catechol (CC) and dopamine (DA). The colorimetric detection was performed by color changes in the TMB solution, while the electrochemical sensing was carried out with SPE modified with MnO2/GQD. The electrochemical technique has shown to be more sensitive toward both analytes, presenting wider linear ranges, lower limits of detection, and reliability for the detection of PCs in real environmental samples. To summarize, our results demonstrate that nanomaterials presenting enzyme-like activities can be a suitable alternative for the easy and sensitive detection of phenolic compounds, which approach can also be extended towards the detection of other emerging pollutants.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no competing interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the financial support from FAPESP (2018/22214-6, 2017/10582-8, 2020/14946-7), CNPq (150002/2021-1), MCTI-SisNano (CNPq/402.287/2013-4), Coordenação de Aperfeiçoamento de Pessoal de Nível Superior - Brasil (CAPES) - Código de Financiamento 001, and Rede Agronano (EMBRAPA). The authors also thank the Laboratory of Structural Characterization (LCE/DEMa/UFSCar) for the microscopy facilities and Dr. Marcelo B. Andrade (IFSC-USP) for the Raman measurements.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.electacta.2022.141777.
